---
title: "Linear MPC"
---

## What "Linear" Means

Linear MPC assumes linear or linearized dynamics and typically quadratic objectives with linear constraints.

A common discrete-time form is:

$$
x_{k+1} = A x_k + B u_k
$$

with optimization variables $u_0, \dots, u_{N-1}$ over horizon $N$.

## Typical Objective Structure

A standard quadratic objective is:

$$
\min \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left(\|x_k - x_k^{\text{ref}}\|_Q^2 + \|u_k\|_R^2\right) + \|x_N - x_N^{\text{ref}}\|_P^2
$$

where $Q, R, P$ are weight matrices.

## Why It Is Popular

- Strong theory and stability tools.
- Efficient QP solvers.
- Good fit when local linearization is accurate.

## Practical Notes

- Model mismatch hurts performance.
- Tuning $Q, R, P$ and horizon length is crucial.
- Constraint scaling and unit consistency matter.

Back to main MPC page: [MPC Overview](mpc.qmd).
